opts: Shoot low hard across the grain
Pass immediately to your open D partner
Skate hard up the boards past the forecheck
Dump it out high off the glass on the strong side
Cycle low and wait for a better chance
Turn around and backcheck hard — immediately
Stay high in the zone to set up the rush back
Pressure the puck carrier deep in their zone
Go to the corner and help your D win the battle
Hold the front of the net — don't leave it empty
Skate to the point to cover the opposing defenseman
Chase the puck carrier behind your own net instead
Yes — pinch down the wall to seal the corner battle and pin the defender along the boards
No — your winger has possession and your half-wall winger can support. Hold the blue line.
Slide over to the half-wall to give your winger an extra short outlet pass to reset the cycle
Back off the blue line and skate deep into the slot area to screen the goalie for the next shot
Charge through the forechecker and try to break the pressure yourself
Make a quick D-to-D pass to your partner — they're open and you're not
Chip the puck off the glass past the forechecker to your winger high up
Skate the puck back behind your own net and wait for a better option up
Both D shift to the strong side and trust the backchecking forwards to cover the weak-side attacker late
One D takes the two on the left, the other holds the middle and takes the weak-side attacker — both communicate
Both D collapse into the slot area and leave the three attackers the entire outside of the zone to work with
Both D stand up tightly at the blue line and try to step up on the strong-side carrier before zone entry is done
Force a seam pass into the slot through the defender who's watching the corner
Shoot from the half-wall — you have time and the slot defender is distracted
Pass back to the point and let your D-man take the shot from up top
Carry the puck below the goal line for a wraparound on the far post
Sprint out to the point to pressure the shooter right away
Move the screener out of the crease — your goalie needs to see
Drop to one knee in front of the goalie to block the shot yourself
Cover the weak-side forward near the hash marks on the power play
Chasing wears out your legs fast, and by the time the puck reaches the slot you have no gas left to block the shot
Chasing breaks the box — gaps open up and the power play gets into the dangerous areas you were protecting
Chasing gives the power play an easy touch pass behind you to reset the whole cycle again with more speed
Chasing puts you out of rhythm with your teammates and the whole PK unit loses its spacing on the ice quickly
Back off toward the hash marks so the goalie can see the shot cleanly and you can chase the long rebound
Tip the puck — even a slight redirection completely changes the trajectory and defeats the goalie's tracking
Screen the goalie by standing tall right in front, but pull your stick back so you never touch the puck at all
Drop into a full shot block at the top of the crease and take the puck in the pads before your goalie sees it
Fake the shot and slide hard across the top of the crease to try to beat the goalie on a clean wraparound attempt
One-time it to the right side where the goalie hasn't moved yet — quick release beats the goalie before they can adjust
Pass cross-ice to the back-door player and let them tap it in at the far short-side post while the goalie is still leaning left
Hold the puck on your backhand and wait for the point D-man to open up a clean lane for the next pass attempt
Force a saucer pass to the back post through all four defenders for a back-door tap-in chance
Pass back to the open point — four defenders below the dots means the point shot is uncontested
Cycle the puck low again and try to pry a defender out of the box formation from below the dots
Drive hard into the slot yourself and try to take the puck through the entire pack for a close shot
Shoot from where you are and crash hard yourself to try and bury your own rebound in tight
Thread the seam pass to the middle player — the PK forward cheating toward you opened that lane
Skate along the wall to the corner to pull the cheating PK forward further out of the box still
Dump the puck low behind the net and try to recover it yourself before the pressure closes in here
Dive headfirst across the shooting lane and block the slap shot cleanly
Get your stick in the shooting lane to deflect or block — you're close enough
Angle the shooter hard to the outside boards and try to force them wide of the net
Peel off to cover the back-door trailer streaking in late for a rebound chance
Drop the puck back to the trailing forward every single time — the trailer always has the best shooting look on a 3-on-2 rush chance
Look at both defenders first — pass to whichever winger the defenders are leaving more open, or drive the middle if it's open
Shoot from the top of the circles right away before either defender can get up to step into the shooting lane to block you
Pass cross-ice to the far winger every single time — cross-ice passes always force the goalie to move across the crease fast
Race into the corner and try to dig the rebound out yourself so the cycle can keep going low along the strong-side boards
Hold your position at the net front unless the puck is about to leave the zone — the power play structure needs you there
Swing up high to the top of the circle so the point D-man has an extra short outlet pass to reset the power play cycle
Slide quickly across to the weak-side post and wait there for a cross-ice feed to come back for a one-time shot attempt
Keep the puck pinned to the boards and let the defender tire themselves out trying to check you off the puck
Wheel to the other side of the net — moving forces the defender to move and may open a teammate
Bank the puck hard off the side boards and chip it up into the neutral zone for your team to regroup
Force a quick pass to your covered teammate and crash hard to the net for a rebound chance yourself
Yes — pull up sharply at the top of the circle and drop the puck back to a trailing teammate for a clean shooting look without the goalie being set at all
No — shoot while moving at full speed. A moving shooter is harder for the goalie to track and slowing down lets the defender catch up.
Yes — slow down and cut sharply across the crease to try and force the goalie to slide hard post to post and open up the far side of the net
Yes — slow down to protect the puck with your body, then carry it behind the net for a clean wraparound attempt on the far post side
Chip the puck off the glass into their zone and forecheck hard — a tied third period is no time to gamble on a long pass through the middle of the ice
Thread the pass to the breaking forward on the left — a tied game in the third is the time to take the calculated risk for a high-danger chance
Take the defended pass to the right instead of risking a long cross-ice feed through traffic when the game is tied in the third period at even strength
Carry the puck wide right yourself and try to beat the defender one-on-one along the boards before dumping it deep for a strong-side forecheck battle
You didn't pinch hard enough down the wall to seal the puck carrier off along the boards before they could chip it past you both and start the 2-on-0
Both D went — one defenseman must always stay back to cover. If your partner is low, you hold the line. If you're pinching, they hold. Never both.
The weak-side winger failed to rotate back to cover for the pinching defenseman and also failed to sprint hard to the net front to stop the 2-on-0
You pinched from the wrong angle and let the puck slip up the boards past your stick — the angle matters most when you jump down from the blue line
The weak-side winger should have rotated down to the slot to cover the sneaking center out front before the pass came out — the winger owns the slot in D-zone coverage
Both D went behind the net — one D battles for the puck, the other covers the front of the net. Leaving the front of the net empty is one of the most dangerous mistakes in hockey.
The goalie should have left the crease to play the puck behind the net before both defensemen had to battle for it — that would have kept a D in front of the empty net
The center should have dropped all the way to the goal line to help both defensemen win the battle — an extra body below the goal line almost always wins the puck battle
The puck carrier slowed down at the wrong moment and killed the team's speed advantage through the neutral zone — you must keep speed on a 3-on-2 at all times on the ice
The two forwards didn't read the puck carrier and entered the zone early — on a rush, everyone times their entry off the puck. If the carrier slows down, you slow down.
The puck carrier should have dumped the puck deep the moment the two forwards crossed the blue line at full speed — that would have kept the play onside and forechecked
The forwards should have curled back to stay onside instead of driving hard into the offensive zone — on a rush the forwards stay even with the puck through the blue line
The other PK forward should have rotated hard across the slot to cover the open hole when their linemate chased the puck behind the net — PK rotations must be automatic every time
The forward left their position in the PK box — on the penalty kill, you stay in structure and cover your zone. Chasing the puck out of position opens the most dangerous area on the ice.
The defensemen should have collapsed hard into the slot to fill the hole the moment the forward left the penalty kill box to chase the puck behind the net on the attack
The goalie should have called the forward off and told them to stay in position inside the penalty kill box instead of chasing the puck behind the crease area at all
The pass came in way too hard and was nearly impossible to handle cleanly on the forehand in such a tight shooting window — a softer pass would have given you time to shoot it
You hesitated — in the slot with a half-second window, you shoot on the catch. Looking down and adjusting gave the goalie time to get set. Quick release beats perfect setup.
You should have redirected the pass directly on net in one motion instead of stopping the puck completely — any deflection off a moving pass is extremely hard for the goalie to track
The goalie read the passing lane way too early and cheated across the crease before the puck even arrived in the slot — you needed a misdirection fake first to freeze them in the blue
The D didn't protect the puck well enough with their body when coming around the net — you have to keep your back to pressure and seal the forechecker off with your body the whole way up
The D skated into pressure instead of away from it — when a forechecker closes from one side, go the other way or use the boards. Skating into pressure is a guaranteed turnover.
The weak-side winger failed to come low to the half-wall to give the D a short outlet pass out of the zone — that low support option would have solved the pressure right away
The D should have reversed the puck behind the net straight to their partner on the other side instead of trying to skate it out — a simple D-to-D reverse beats a hard forecheck
The D should have angled the attacker hard to the boards at the blue line to force a dump-in — angling is how you control a 1-on-1 without having to commit to a poke check too early
The D gave up too much ice — backing up to the crease gives the attacker time, space, and options. Close the gap at the blue line and take away their time to make plays.
The partner D should have stepped up hard at the red line to force a turnover before the zone entry ever happened — good gap control starts in the neutral zone, not at the blue
The forwards should have backchecked much harder to take away the attacker's support options through the neutral zone — backcheckers are the first layer of gap control on every rush
The two forwards should have dumped the puck deep before leaving the offensive zone for their change — dumping it in first gives your team time to change safely and stops the counter
The tired forwards changed too slowly at the wrong time — after a turnover, you sprint back first, then change when the puck is safe. Changing during transition is how you give up odd-man goals.
The defensemen should have held the blue line much longer and stepped up aggressively to slow the 4-on-3 rush coming at them — holding the line forces attackers to make a tough play
The fresh forwards on the bench should have jumped over the boards the instant the puck was turned over to fill the open ice — a quick change can work like a backcheck if the exchange is clean
The forwards keep cycling the puck low on the strong side and keep trying to pry a defender out of the box formation — cycling low eventually creates a mistake
The weak-side D has an open shooting lane or passing option — the defense was overloaded to the strong side and the D-to-D pass shifts the play to open ice
The strong-side D-man curls back to the blue line to take a fast reset pass up from below the dots — resetting at the blue line holds the zone possession safely
The defense slides all the way across perfectly in time and matches up again on the weak side with no opening for a shot or pass at all on the power play unit
The puck carrier snaps a quick wrist shot from the top of the circles and crashes hard to the net for their own rebound before the D tie them up in front
The puck carrier drops it back to the trailing D who has a clean shot from the top of the circles — the defense never accounted for the trailer
Both wingers crash the posts hard while the puck carrier tries to deke the goalie one-on-one in tight for a dangerous high-skill scoring chance in close traffic
The puck carrier fires a quick cross-ice pass to the far winger for a one-timer shot against the grain before the defenders can close down the middle lane
Your center skates the puck hard up the strong-side boards and tries to carry it all the way into the offensive zone alone without a single pass to anyone
Your center headmans it to a winger breaking up ice — the other team is caught deep, and a quick pass to a winger with speed creates an odd-man rush the other way
Your center chips the puck high off the glass and out of the zone for a quick line change since the D-zone is already clear and a tired line needs fresh legs
Your center holds the puck and waits for the D to join the rush before starting a breakout pass up to a winger — patience usually beats a rushed pass into traffic
The PK forwards ease up and drop back into the box formation since they already got the clear — the kill is about staying fresh, not pressuring at the blue line every time
Your PK forwards pressure the D and force a turnover or a bad re-entry — aggressive PK forechecking at the blue line disrupts the PP's rhythm
The power play uses the extra skater to walk the puck in cleanly through the neutral zone and set up in the offensive zone with no real pressure from the penalty kill
The power play regroups quickly in the neutral zone and threads a stretch pass behind the PK forecheckers for a clean zone entry and a fast breakaway on the goalie
The weak-side D-man skates out of the zone and tries to carry the puck up ice alone to get away from all of the pressure — moving the puck with your feet is the safest play after a faceoff loss
Your forwards and D collapse to the net, box out opposing players, and either clear the puck or help the goalie freeze it — loose pucks in front after a faceoff loss require an all-hands response
The wingers sprint hard to the blue line looking for a long stretch pass since the D-zone is already lost here — stretching the ice forces the other team to back off the forecheck and gives space
The center swings up high to the top of the circles to give whoever has the puck a short outlet pass option — centers are the safety valve on every single breakout play in the defensive zone
You pull the puck to your backhand and try to tuck it in at the short-side post before the goalie can recover to the post
You lift the puck high over the butterfly — goalie is down, the top of the net is wide open, and you have the puck and time to elevate it
You slide a quick pass across the crease to a teammate on the far post for a tap-in since the goalie is already down low
You pull up sharply and take the puck back to the top of the circle to reset the offensive zone play from up high
You keep driving hard to the outside and take away the outside lane all the way down to the goal line because that's your original angle on the whole rush
You pivot hard and recover to the inside — the forward just beat your angle, and if you don't recover inside, they'll have the slot. Pivot feet, close the inside lane.
You step up and throw a heavy body check on the forward the moment they cut back to the middle of the ice — a hard hit at that spot breaks the play up completely
You drop down to block the cross-ice pass and trust your goalie to handle the forward one-on-one in tight — taking away the pass is often more important than the shooter
Hold your depth on the strong side and challenge the half-wall shooter aggressively before they can pass the puck across — cutting the angle on the puck comes first
Push across the crease aggressively using a butterfly slide or T-push — the backdoor tap-in is the highest-danger chance and you need to close that post
Back deep into the crease and stay square to the puck carrier along the half-wall boards on the strong side — depth gives you time to react to shot or pass
Stand tall on the strong-side post and reach across with your stick to try and take away the back-door passing lane without pushing across the full crease
